name: Electric Motor
tagline: Magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors producing continuous rotary torque without on-site combustion
problem:
Before electricity, motive power in a workshop meant leather belts snaking from a ceiling shaft, or a small steam engine hissing beside each heavy machine. Belts slip, alignments drift, and every new tool demands another hole in the ceiling. An electric motor replaces that tangle with a compact drum of copper and iron that pulls power through a wire — the fire stays at the power station while the work happens where it is needed.

Motors are not magic rotation boxes; they are heat machines that happen to spin. Current in the windings meets resistance; iron reverses magnetisation sixty times a second; bearings rub. Neglect cooling and the insulation class becomes a countdown. Start a large motor on a weak line and the whole village’s lights dip — inrush currents can be many times running current until the rotor catches up with the magnetic field.

Variable speed was historically expensive: rheostats turned excess energy into smoke. Modern drives synthesise new frequencies electronically, but introduce harmonics that can pit bearing races through tiny capacitive currents. The motor problem is therefore electrical, mechanical, and thermal at once.

For civilisation recovery, motors are precious because they couple precision metallurgy (laminations), chemistry (varnish and enamel), and craft (winding) into one dependable worker that never sleeps.
overview:
Most industrial motors you meet are induction machines: the stator’s three-phase windings create a magnetic field that appears to rotate around the rotor. Conductors in the rotor — often aluminium bars cast into a steel stack — see that motion as a changing field and develop currents of their own. Those currents create their own field that tries to catch the stator’s field, producing torque. The rotor must slip slightly behind synchronous speed; at perfect synchronism induction would stop and torque would vanish.

Laminated silicon steel in the stator guides flux while limiting eddy currents. Slots hold insulated copper or aluminium conductors; the slot shape trades ease of manufacture against stray loss. The frame and fan path move heat from windings to air; larger machines may use water jackets or external blowers.

Synchronous motors lock to grid frequency once running; they can also behave as generators. DC motors still appear where fine torque control predates cheap electronics — brushes wear but the physics is transparent. Universal motors in power tools accept AC or DC at the cost of sparking brushes and shorter life.

Nameplate letters and efficiency bands encode starting behaviour: some rotor designs prioritise high starting torque for compressors; others prioritise low inrush for weak grids. Reading the nameplate is as important as reading a steam plate’s pressure rating.

principles.name: Lorentz force
principles.explanation: Current crossing magnetic field experiences force — the microscopic origin of motor torque.

principles.name: Slip in induction machines
principles.explanation: Rotor must lag synchronous speed to induce rotor current; at synchronous speed torque collapses to zero.

principles.name: Back-EMF
principles.explanation: Spinning conductors generate voltage opposing applied voltage — limits no-load current and sets constant-speed behaviour on stiff grids.

principles.name: Thermal class
principles.explanation: Insulation life halves roughly every 10°C over rated — derating altitude and ambient is mandatory.

components.name: Stator core and windings
components.madeFrom: Silicon steel laminations, magnet wire, varnish

components.name: Rotor and shaft
components.function: Converts magnetic torque to mechanical output
components.madeFrom: Aluminium or copper cage, steel shaft

components.name: Bearings and end bells
components.function: Supports rotor; seals windings from environment
components.madeFrom: Ball or sleeve bearings; cast aluminium bells

components.name: Terminal box
components.function: Connects supply to windings; may include star-delta links

components.name: Fan and cooling path
components.function: Removes copper and iron losses as heat

rawMaterials.name: Electrical steel
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Ferrite or powdered-iron cores at high frequency for small actuators; lower-flux-density silicon stacks where grain-oriented sheet is unavailable; import lamination stacks from surviving terrestrial mills until local rolling lines return

rawMaterials.name: Magnet wire
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Silver-plated copper in cryogenic motors; aluminium magnet wire in large machines where slot fill and cost dominate
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Random wound or form wound coils

rawMaterials.name: Aluminium for rotors
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Copper cage bars for higher efficiency when aluminium casting equipment is missing; hand-fabricated copper rings for repair of legacy rotors
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Die casting under vacuum to reduce porosity

buildSteps.title: Choose the motor type to match your supply and job
buildSteps.description: If you have stable three-phase AC, a squirrel-cage induction motor is the default workhorse. If you only have single-phase domestic supply, look for split-phase or capacitor-start designs — understand that starting capacitors are not interchangeable decoration. If you need speed control, plan for a variable-frequency drive and shielded cable suitable for fast switching edges.
buildSteps.warningNote: A motor rated for delta on 400 V will misbehave if you star-connect it without reading the plate

buildSteps.title: Stack laminations and insert slot liners without burrs
buildSteps.description: Interleave stator laminations and weld or cleat the back iron per your design. Slide slot liners in so conductors never touch steel directly. Deburr every lamination tooth — a burr is a short waiting to happen. Blow out grit before windings enter; grit abrades enamel during vibration.
buildSteps.warningNote: Steel edges after punching are razor sharp

buildSteps.title: Wind or insert coils, then varnish or vacuum-pressure impregnate
buildSteps.description: Maintain even tension while winding magnet wire, or press pre-formed coils into slots if using form-wound construction. Tie coils at the end turns so magnetic forces cannot move them. After winding, varnish dip or VPI fills voids so moisture cannot creep along strands.
buildSteps.warningNote: Solvent vapours are flammable — ventilate and kill ignition sources

buildSteps.title: Assemble rotor, balance, and align to the driven machine
buildSteps.description: For fabricated rotors, verify cage integrity and skew. Machine the outside diameter if casting left ovality. Balance dynamically to the ISO grade appropriate for speed — an unbalanced rotor walks bearings out in weeks. Bolt the motor to a flat base; check soft foot with feeler gauges under each foot while tightening in a cross pattern. Align coupling to load within a few hundredths of a millimetre; repeat hot if thermal growth matters.
buildSteps.warningNote: Coupling guard removed for alignment must be replaced before first spin

buildSteps.title: Megger, spin no-load, then load-test thermally
buildSteps.description: Measure insulation resistance phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground with a megohmmeter at a voltage appropriate to the machine class. Spin at no load listening for rubs; measure current — wildly high no-load current means shorted turns or wrong connections. Apply load gradually; watch bearing temperature rise for an hour; capture vibration if you have a phone app or accelerometer. Document baseline numbers for the next maintainer.
buildSteps.warningNote: Shafts and fan blades remain dangerous above idle speed — guard before energising

history.event: First practical rotating electric motors demonstrated in laboratory contexts.
Tesla polyphase induction motor patents underpin modern cage motors.
Three-phase transmission in Frankfurt links motors to remote generation.

inventors.name: Nikola Tesla
inventors.contribution: Polyphase induction and AC motor concepts

impact:
Motors made electricity actionable at every machine scale — from dental drills to ship propulsion. They are the distributed muscle of electrification.

Recovery must preserve lamination stamping and varnish impregnation, not only copper.

videos.title: Induction motor principles
videos.description: Accredited electrical engineering animations of rotating fields and slip.
